content.text: JSX Is Not HTML — It Is Syntax Sugar
JSX looks like HTML inside JavaScript, but the browser cannot run it directly. JSX is a
— your build tool (Babel, SWC, TypeScript) converts every JSX expression into a plain JavaScript function call before the code reaches the browser or Node.js.
from the automatic JSX runtime). Every element you write in JSX becomes one call to this function, returning a plain JavaScript object called a
JSX is not required to use React. Everything you can write in JSX you can write as
calls. JSX exists purely for readability.

content.text: The Babel plugin (or SWC / TypeScript compiler with
A string for HTML elements ('div', 'span', 'button') or a reference to a component function/class for custom components.
An object containing all attributes and event handlers. null when no props. The children prop can also be included here.
Zero or more children — strings, numbers, other React elements, arrays. Multiple children become multiple arguments.
does not touch the DOM. It returns a plain JavaScript object — the
— that describes what should be rendered:

This object is immutable — React never mutates it. It is a snapshot description of what the UI should look like at a point in time. React uses it to build and diff the fiber tree before touching the DOM.

field exists to prevent XSS attacks. A plain JSON object fetched from a server cannot contain a Symbol, so React rejects objects that appear to be elements but lack this field. If you inject an arbitrary object into JSX and it happens to have the right shape, React will reject it as unsafe.
calls — the children of each element are resolved first:
Each nested element becomes its own
call. The result is a tree of plain objects — the virtual DOM. React walks this tree during the render phase to compute what should change in the real DOM.
When the JSX tag starts with an uppercase letter, Babel treats it as a reference to a JavaScript value (a function or class), not an HTML element string:
This is why custom components must be capitalized in JSX — if you write
, telling React to create a native HTML button. If you write
function reference, telling React to call that function.
The Classic vs Automatic JSX Runtime
React 17 introduced a new JSX transform. The compiler output differs between the two:
import React from 'react' in every file with JSX
No import needed — compiler adds it
In props or as extra arg

The automatic transform removes the need to import React in every file. The practical behavior for your components is identical — the difference is invisible at the React level. Vite and Create React App both use the automatic transform by default since they target React 17+.
JSX supports the spread operator for props, which compiles to
or spread in the props object:
Dynamic attribute values are JavaScript expressions inside
. The compiler passes them directly as values in the props object — no additional quoting or escaping:
JSX has specific rules about how children are handled during compilation:
prop: if there is one child it is the child directly, if there are multiple they are in an array. Your components can check
Understanding the compilation makes it possible to write React without any JSX — useful in environments without a build step or for programmatic UI generation:
This is exactly what Babel produces from JSX. The verbosity is why JSX was invented — the two representations are semantically identical.
The chain from JSX to the screen is:
Your component function calls createElement (implicitly via JSX) and returns a tree of React elements.
React's reconciler compares this element tree with the previous tree to find differences.
React updates only the DOM nodes that need to change, not the entire tree.
After the commit, useEffect and useLayoutEffect callbacks run.

React elements are lightweight — creating thousands of them per render is cheap. The element objects are not DOM nodes; they are descriptions that React uses to decide what the DOM should look like. The expense of rendering comes from the reconciliation and DOM mutation steps, not from creating the element objects.
Key and Ref Are Not Props
— are extracted by the compiler and placed outside the props object:
This is why trying to read
— they are stripped from the props object before the component receives them. Pass your own custom prop if you need to forward the key value to a child.
JSX is syntactic sugar that the compiler (Babel, SWC, TypeScript) rewrites into

calls. Each JSX element becomes one function call that returns a plain JavaScript object — the React element — describing type, props, key, ref, and children. Lowercase tags become string types (host elements); uppercase tags pass component function references. The resulting element tree is a cheap description of the UI; React's reconciler uses it to compute minimal DOM updates. Understanding this compilation chain demystifies how JSX works, why capitalization matters, and why
JSX trims and compresses whitespace differently from HTML. Understanding the rules prevents surprising empty text nodes:
renders the number 0 because 0 is falsy but not
JSX requires a single root element. When you need to return multiple sibling elements without a wrapper DOM node, use
Fragments produce no DOM node — they are invisible wrappers. The resulting DOM has
(not the shorthand) when you need a key inside a list of fragments.
JSX is JavaScript — you can use any JavaScript expression inside
, and JSX can appear anywhere a JavaScript expression is valid:
accepts expressions, not statements. You cannot put
directly inside JSX — but you can call a function that contains them, use ternaries, or use logical operators.
Configuring the JSX Transform in Your Project
The JSX transform is configured in your build tooling and TypeScript compiler:
in tsconfig (automatic runtime), you never need to write
in files that only use JSX. The compiler injects the necessary import automatically. You still need to import React for
, and other APIs — just not for JSX syntax itself.
creates a new React element from scratch,
creates a copy of an existing element with optional prop overrides:

is less common today — context and composition patterns (render props, component composition) usually accomplish the same goal without manipulating the element directly. But it remains useful for legacy slot-based APIs.

JSX compiles to React.createElement(type, props, ...children). Lowercase tags produce string types; uppercase tags pass component references. createElement returns a plain object, not a DOM node. key and ref are extracted to the top level of the element, not in props. The automatic JSX runtime (React 17+) eliminates the need to import React in every file with JSX.
